caveats: a: BLS data are NAICS 322 rather than 322219 or the narrowed lens.
a: The retained product groups may have different task mixes despite greater coherence than the full code.
rho: No representative implementation evidence for the narrowed population was found.
rho: Integration cost may be disproportionately high for small custom manufacturers.
e: No source gives the revenue or firm share of each 322219 product group.
e: The injected n=67 is an ESTIMATE using a broad industry margin and may not fit each subgroup.
s5: Owner-age evidence is all-industry and from data year 2018.
s5: The narrowed population may be more family-owned than the employer-business average.
q: No representative contract sample was found.
q: Retention is likely higher in decorated setup boxes and lower in standardized industrial cores.
d5: No direct demand series for the narrowed basket was found.
d5: The retained end markets are diverse and can move in opposite directions.
o: Standard mailing tubes or cores may face more self-service and distributor substitution than bespoke products.
o: Material substitution and end-market contraction are captured in d5.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat business-to-business programs for specification-defined rigid boxes and specialty fiber containers, sold through quotes, purchase orders, releases, and supply agreements with pricing based on materials, dimensions, printing or finishing, tooling, run length, and freight.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 322219 combines setup boxes and industrial fiber tubes, cans, and drums with sanitary food containers, cups, plates, and milk cartons. The lens retains specialty setup-box and industrial fiber-container manufacturing because those share custom, repeat, specification-led production; high-volume sanitary and milk-container operations are excluded because their food-contact compliance, production scale, and commodity customer model make a single screen incoherent.
lens.excluded_activities: High-volume sanitary food containers, paper cups and plates, and milk cartons; captive plants; paperboard mills; folding or corrugated boxes; brokers without accountable production; shells; financing vehicles; and firms outside the EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: Independent US manufacturers in the approximately $1-10 million normalized EBITDA band repeatedly supplying custom setup paperboard boxes and specialty fiber tubes, cores, cans, drums, mailing cases, reels, and similar containers to external industrial and consumer customers.

a.bridge: BLS occupation counts cover all paper manufacturing, while the target is a narrowed 322219 specialty-container lens. The broader production-heavy mix was translated by treating quoting, specification intake, artwork, scheduling, purchasing, quality records, maintenance support, and customer service as exposed; the proxy may understate information work in short-run custom plants and overstate it where ERP and design tools already automate those steps.
caveats: BLS data are NAICS 322 rather than 322219 or the narrowed lens.
The retained product groups may have different task mixes despite greater coherence than the full code.
a.change_evidence: Payroll-weighted task studies from setup-box and fiber-container plants, including present CAD, estimating, ERP, vision, and scheduling automation and manual hours by occupation and workflow.
a.rationale: Physical winding, cutting, forming, wrapping, printing, assembly, material handling, maintenance, and inspection dominate production. Custom geometries, finishes, tooling, repeated estimates, job schedules, and documentation create a meaningful but minority pool of AI-addressable work.

d5.bridge: BLS projects 1.7% annual real-output growth for all converted paper products, not the narrowed 322219 service basket; AF&PA reports upstream boxboard rather than specialty-container shipments. Moderating the broader forecast reflects mixed end markets, but can overstate declining industrial uses or understate premium packaging and paper substitution.
caveats: No direct demand series for the narrowed basket was found.
The retained end markets are diverse and can move in opposite directions.
d5.change_evidence: Shipment histories and five-year forecasts separately for setup boxes, fiber cores and tubes, fiber drums, mailing tubes, and composite cans, reconciled to customer industry output, substitution, lightweighting, and domestic capacity.
d5.rationale: BLS expects converted-paper-product real output to rise through 2034, while AF&PA reports boxboard production essentially flat in 2025. Specialty setup boxes can benefit from premium and presentation packaging, and tubes and cores track diverse industrial production; the central case is modest growth with a wide range.

caveats: No source gives the revenue or firm share of each 322219 product group.
The injected n=67 is an ESTIMATE using a broad industry margin and may not fit each subgroup.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level product and ownership census of all frozen-band 322219 firms, allocating revenue among setup boxes, fiber tubes and drums, sanitary containers, cups and plates, and milk cartons, with captive status, external revenue, EBITDA, and customer concentration.
e.rationale: The narrowed product groups usually provide repeat external production and can be transferable stand-alone businesses. Eligibility is reduced materially because the frozen n spans sanitary food and milk-container manufacturers excluded for coherence, as well as captive, integrated, customer-dependent, or mis-sized operations.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 322219 ($1,832,627,273) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 322219 ($9,011,828,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1465 (margins.json[32]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Paper/Forest Products EBITDA/Sales 14.65% (mid-range for wood/paper/plastics)); revenue/employee $459,201 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 322219); class <5: 25 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,427,840 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $209,179 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 11 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,450,273 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $212,465 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 28 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,153,107 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $608,430 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 54 firms, avg revenue/firm $11,721,556 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,717,208 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 13 firms, avg revenue/firm $44,549,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,526,428 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 22 firms, avg revenue/firm $346,899,591 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $50,820,790 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 67

caveats: Standard mailing tubes or cores may face more self-service and distributor substitution than bespoke products.
Material substitution and end-market contraction are captured in d5.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of customers adopting in-house tube, core, drum, or rigid-box production at scale, or autonomous plants operating without accountable quality and production management.
o.rationale: Rigid setup boxes and fiber containers are physical products requiring accountable design translation, forming, assembly, quality control, maintenance, and delivery. Digital ordering and automated converting can reduce labor but do not replace the manufacturer; only limited customer in-sourcing is plausible within five years.

caveats: No representative contract sample was found.
Retention is likely higher in decorated setup boxes and lower in standardized industrial cores.
q.change_evidence: Contract and quote-level data by retained product group showing raw-material escalators, renewal cadence, cost-down clauses, bid competition, customer switching, and margin persistence after productivity improvements.
q.rationale: Custom dimensions, decoration, tooling, short runs, application knowledge, and service can obscure and protect operational gains. Material-price adjustments, rebids, larger industrial buyers, and repeat-program negotiations still share benefits, especially for standardized tubes and cores.

caveats: No representative implementation evidence for the narrowed population was found.
Integration cost may be disproportionately high for small custom manufacturers.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after operational audits of specialty-container AI deployments showing labor hours, quote speed, specification errors, scrap, setup time, on-time delivery, complaint rates, and human-review burden.
rho.rationale: AI-assisted quoting, specification parsing, artwork checks, schedule support, procurement, maintenance retrieval, quality drafting, and service triage are implementable within five years. Small-firm systems, bespoke exceptions, physical sample approvals, legacy equipment, and human quality accountability limit realized deployment.

s5.bridge: Census measures ages of responding employer-business owners across all industries rather than five-year transfers in specialty paperboard containers. A fraction of the 51% age-55-plus population plus normal strategic sales is translated into qualifying transfers after excluding internal succession, closure, and unsaleable operations; small family ownership may make the proxy overstate sales, while packaging consolidation may make it understate them.
caveats: Owner-age evidence is all-industry and from data year 2018.
The narrowed population may be more family-owned than the employer-business average.
s5.change_evidence: A ten-year database of closed, failed, and withdrawn transactions for US setup-box and fiber-container manufacturers in the band, supplemented by current owner-age and succession-plan interviews.
s5.rationale: Aging owners create succession pressure, and recurring specialty accounts plus equipment and know-how support transfer. Bespoke owner relationships, customer concentration, equipment needs, and family succession prevent many potential events from becoming qualifying sales.

principal_driver: Custom, repeat specifications and higher labor intensity create useful AI opportunities in estimating, design support, scheduling, quality, and customer workflows.
principal_weakness: The NAICS code mixes fundamentally different container businesses, leaving eligibility and demand highly uncertain after narrowing.

sources.supports: BLS reports 2025 paper-manufacturing employment of 87,050 paper-goods machine operators, 17,540 production supervisors, 14,210 industrial-truck operators, and 6,640 production managers, supporting a production-heavy occupation mix.
sources.title: Paper Manufacturing: NAICS 322

sources.supports: Census defines the industry as converting purchased paperboard into containers other than corrugated, solid-fiber, and folding boxes, with illustrative examples including fiber cans and drums, milk cartons, sanitary food containers, and setup boxes.
sources.title: 322219: Other Paperboard Container Manufacturing - Census Bureau Profile

sources.supports: BLS projects converted paper product manufacturing output increasing from $99.0 billion in 2024 to $116.9 billion in 2034 in chained 2017 dollars, a 1.7% compound annual rate.
sources.title: Employment and output by industry, 2024-2034

sources.supports: AF&PA reports 2025 boxboard production essentially flat at 12.4 million tons while overall US paper and paperboard production declined 3.7%; survey data represent about 87% of industry capacity with estimates completing the dataset.
sources.title: AF&PA Releases 66th Annual Paper Industry Capacity and Fiber Consumption Survey

sources.supports: The 2019 Annual Business Survey infographic reports 51% of responding owners of employer businesses were age 55 or older; estimates represent 4.1 million responding owners in data year 2018.
sources.title: Business Owners' Ages

title: Other Paperboard Container Manufacturing
